images.caption:
Figure 1:Overall pipeline of ContactPrompt.Given an input image𝐈\mathbf{I}and text prompt𝐓(0)\mathbf{T}^{(0)}, we first perform free-form reasoning with MLLMs to produce a global interaction description𝐳\mathbf{z}.
Next, part-level contact prediction is performed using𝐈\mathbf{I},𝐳\mathbf{z}, a text prompt𝐓(1)\mathbf{T}^{(1)}, and hand part segmentation𝐒part\mathbf{S}_{\text{part}}to obtain predicted contact parts𝒫^\hat{\mathcal{P}}.
Moreover, dense vertex-level contact is estimated by providing𝐈\mathbf{I},𝐓(2)\mathbf{T}^{(2)},𝒫^\hat{\mathcal{P}},𝐳\mathbf{z}, the full visual prompt𝐒full\mathbf{S}_{\text{full}}, and the part-wise vertex grid specification𝐐𝒫^\mathbf{Q}_{\hat{\mathcal{P}}}, producing part-wise grid outputs𝐆^\hat{\mathbf{G}}.
Lastly, the output𝐆^\hat{\mathbf{G}}is mapped to final dense hand contact map.
Figure 2:Comparison of hand part segmentation definition with DIGITFanet al.(2021).Our ContactPrompt provides more detailed hand part segmentation that is aligned with the function of hand parts.

Figure 3:Details of visual prompt in ContactPrompt.The visual prompt consists of hand part indices and part-wise vertex grids.
Hand part indices associate each region with its numeric label.
The vertex grid shows row structure, where each row starts with a dot, vertices are connected by lines, and consecutive rows are linked to indicate sequential ordering between rows of the grid.

Figure 4:Qualitative comparison of dense hand contact estimation with BSTROHuanget al.(2022), DECOTripathiet al.(2023), HACOJung and Lee (2025)on MOWCaoet al.(2021)dataset.We emphasize exemplar regions, where ContactPrompt outperforms previous methods, in red circles.
